reference_block.name: purpose
reference_block.title: Purpose
reference_block.statement: This module defines how to create a new role file using the native XML role format.
A role module defines activation, remit, boundaries, collaboration expectations, evidence discipline, and completion checks for one discipline or operating mode.
A role module is not a converted document. It is a semantic prompt asset.

reference_block.name: file-identity
reference_block.title: File Identity and Naming
reference_block.statement: Each role module must be stored in the roles directory and named with the discipline identifier used by mode triggers and registries.

checklist.entry: Use lowercase kebab case, for example: user-research.xml, content-strategy.xml, ethical-ai.xml.
Use the discipline or role name, not an individual practitioner title unless that is the canonical role identifier.
The root element id attribute should match the filename stem.
The version attribute should change when the role behaviour changes materially.

reference_block.name: root-structure
reference_block.title: Root Structure
reference_block.statement: Every role file must use role_module as the root element.

role_definition.persona: Describe the role in one sentence.

does_not_own.capability: List what this role does not own.

binding.interaction: State how the role uses or contributes to the primitive.

evidence_type.name: Formal Evidence
evidence_type.description: Describe strong evidence for this role.

prohibited.constraint: State what the role must not do.

permitted.constraint: State what the role is expected to do.

phase.name: Discovery
phase.contribution: State how the role contributes in this phase.

quality_gates.gate: State the checks that outputs from this role must satisfy.

reference_block.name: required-elements
reference_block.title: Required Elements
reference_block.statement: The following elements are required in every role module:

checklist.entry: title
activation
role_definition
scope
shared_primitive_bindings
principles
evidence_discipline
constraints
phase_contributions
quality_gates
output_contract

reference_block.statement: These required elements define how the role activates, what it is for, what it owns, how it reasons, and how its outputs are judged.

reference_block.name: activation-model
reference_block.title: Activation Model
reference_block.statement: The activation block declares how the role becomes active.

checklist.entry: Use a single trigger element when one command activates the role.
If the role can activate through more than one alias, use multiple trigger elements.
Do not describe activation only in prose. Encode it explicitly.

reference_block.name: role-definition
reference_block.title: Role Definition
reference_block.statement: The role_definition block should explain the role briefly and operationally.

checklist.entry: Use persona to identify the role.
Use mission to define the role's objective.
Avoid long narrative paragraphs.
Describe what the role contributes in multidisciplinary delivery.

reference_block.name: scope-model
reference_block.title: Scope Model
reference_block.statement: The scope block defines authority boundaries.

checklist.entry: Use owns for capabilities, responsibilities, and decisions the role is expected to carry.
Use does_not_own for capabilities, responsibilities, and decisions outside the role's authority.
Make boundaries explicit so the role does not absorb adjacent disciplines by accident.

reference_block.name: shared-primitives
reference_block.title: Shared Primitive Bindings
reference_block.statement: Each role must state how it contributes to or consumes the shared reasoning primitives used across the bundle.

checklist.entry: Create one binding per primitive that matters to the role.
Use the primitive attribute to name the shared primitive exactly.
Use interaction to explain the role's contribution, interpretation, or dependency.
Do not use a document-style table as the primary representation.

reference_block.name: principles-model
reference_block.title: Principles Model
reference_block.statement: The principles block defines the durable operating rules that guide the role.

checklist.entry: Each principle should be brief, stable, and action-guiding.
Use priority when a principle is especially important.
Prefer principle elements over a long prose section.

reference_block.name: evidence-discipline
reference_block.title: Evidence Discipline
reference_block.statement: Every role must state what counts as evidence and how confidence should be handled.

checklist.entry: Describe Formal Evidence for the discipline.
Describe Partner Input from collaborating disciplines.
Describe Unvalidated Input such as assumptions or early-stage reasoning.
State that low-confidence or unvalidated inputs must be flagged.

reference_block.name: constraints-model
reference_block.title: Constraints Model
reference_block.statement: The constraints block defines prohibited and permitted behaviour.

checklist.entry: Use prohibited for behaviours the role must not perform.
Use permitted for behaviours the role is expected or allowed to perform.
Use severity for critical prohibitions.
Do not bury role boundaries in narrative paragraphs.

reference_block.name: phase-contributions-model
reference_block.title: Phase Contributions Model
reference_block.statement: The phase_contributions block describes how the role contributes across service phases.

checklist.entry: Use one phase element per relevant phase.
Typical phase names are Pre-Discovery, Discovery, Alpha, Beta, and Live.
Use contribution elements rather than generic bullet lists wherever possible.
If the role does not materially contribute in a phase, omit that phase rather than inventing filler.

reference_block.name: quality-gates-model
reference_block.title: Quality Gates Model
reference_block.statement: The quality_gates block states what must be true before outputs from the role are treated as complete or dependable.

checklist.entry: Each gate should be testable or reviewable.
Avoid vague gates such as good quality or appropriate detail.
Prefer concrete checks such as evidence cited, boundary respected, risk surfaced, or accessibility considered.

reference_block.name: optional-elements
reference_block.title: Optional Elements
reference_block.statement: Additional semantic blocks may be added where they strengthen role behaviour and remain stable across time.

checklist.entry: tool_policy
interaction_policy
decision_policy
collaboration
handoff
examples
checks

reference_block.statement: Only use optional elements when they carry semantic value. Do not add them as decoration.

checklist.entry: Write semantic XML, not Markdown disguised as XML.
Do not use paragraph, section, list, and item as the primary architecture.
Use dedicated element names that express intent.
Keep statements short and operational.
Prefer typed child elements over mixed prose.
Do not include provenance metadata such as converted_from or source_file in canonical role modules.
Keep cross-file references limited to real dependencies such as shared primitives or output contracts.
Copying old Markdown headings into title attributes.
Embedding fenced code blocks to show former syntax.
Using generic document tags as the main semantic layer.
Writing long descriptive essays instead of operational rules.
Mixing what the role is, what it does, and what it must never do in one untyped paragraph.
Including instructions that belong in templates, graders, or references instead of the role itself.

reference_block.name: completion-check
reference_block.title: Completion Check
reference_block.statement: A new role module is ready when:

checklist.entry: The module activates through an explicit trigger.
The remit and boundaries are unambiguous.
The shared primitive bindings are explicit.
The evidence discipline is defined.
The phase contributions are meaningful.
The quality gates are testable.
The module points to the output contract.
The module reads like an operational constitution, not a converted handbook page.
